questions.stem: Let $f : X \to Y$ be a map of topological spaces. The topological definition of continuity says $f$ is continuous when which condition holds?
options: $f$ maps open sets to open sets.
For every open set $V \subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$.
$f$ maps closed sets to closed sets.
questions.explanation: The standard definition: $f$ is continuous iff the preimage of every open set is open. Mapping open sets to open sets is the definition of an open map, a different property, and bijectivity is unrelated to continuity.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which condition on a map $f : X \to Y$ is equivalent to continuity?
options: For every closed set $C \subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed in $X$.
$f$ maps every closed set to a closed set.
questions.explanation: Continuity is equivalent to the preimage of every closed set being closed, since $f^{-1}(Y \setminus C) = X \setminus f^{-1}(C)$ converts the open-preimage condition into the closed-preimage condition. The other options concern injectivity or images, not continuity.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A homeomorphism between topological spaces $X$ and $Y$ is a map $f : X \to Y$ that is which of the following?
options: A continuous bijection whose inverse $f^{-1}$ is also continuous.
questions.explanation: A homeomorphism is a bijection $f$ such that both $f$ and $f^{-1}$ are continuous. A continuous bijection alone need not be a homeomorphism (its inverse can fail to be continuous), so the two-sided continuity is essential.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Two spaces $X$ and $Y$ are called homeomorphic when which of the following is true?
options: They have the same number of points.
There is a continuous surjection $X \to Y$.
There exists a homeomorphism between them.
questions.explanation: Spaces are homeomorphic exactly when some homeomorphism exists between them; this is the topological notion of equivalence. Equal cardinality or a mere continuous surjection is far weaker and does not imply homeomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Given a subset $A$ of a space $X$, the subspace topology on $A$ consists of which sets?
options: The sets $U \cap A$ where $U$ is open in $X$.
Only the sets that are open in $X$ and contained in $A$.
The closed subsets of $X$ intersected with $A$.
questions.explanation: The subspace (relative) topology on $A$ has as its open sets exactly the intersections $U \cap A$ with $U$ open in $X$. These need not be open in $X$ itself; openness is measured within $A$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: On the product $X \times Y$, the product topology has as a basis which collection?
options: The sets $U \times V$ with $U$ open in $X$ and $V$ open in $Y$.
All subsets $S \times T$ with $S \subseteq X$, $T \subseteq Y$.
The sets $U \times Y$ with $U$ open in $X$, alone.
questions.explanation: The product topology is generated by the basis of rectangles $U \times V$ where $U$ and $V$ are open in the factors. Arbitrary products $S \times T$ of any subsets do not form the product topology in general.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A map $f : X \to Y$ into a product space, say $f = (f_1, f_2) : X \to Y_1 \times Y_2$, is continuous if and only if which holds?
options: At least one coordinate function is continuous.
Each coordinate function $f_1$ and $f_2$ is continuous.
questions.explanation: A map into a product (with the product topology) is continuous exactly when every coordinate function is continuous. This universal property is one of the defining features of the product topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The projection map $\pi_1 : X \times Y \to X$ from a product (with the product topology) onto a factor has which property?
options: It is closed but not continuous.
It is neither continuous nor open.
It is a homeomorphism in general.
It is continuous and open (an open map).
questions.explanation: Projections from a product are continuous (preimages of open sets are open rectangles) and open (the image of a basic open rectangle $U \times V$ is the open set $U$). In general they are not homeomorphisms, since they are usually not injective.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Any constant map $f : X \to Y$ (sending every point to a fixed $y_0 \in Y$) is which of the following?
options: Continuous for every topology on $X$ and $Y$.
questions.explanation: For a constant map, the preimage of any open set $V$ is either all of $X$ (if $y_0 \in V$) or $\emptyset$ (if not), both of which are open in any topology. So a constant map is always continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: If $X$ carries the discrete topology, which statement about maps $f : X \to Y$ into any space $Y$ is correct?
questions.explanation: When $X$ is discrete, every subset of $X$ is open, so $f^{-1}(V)$ is automatically open for every $V$. Hence every map out of a discrete space is continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The composition of two continuous maps $f : X \to Y$ and $g : Y \to Z$ is which of the following?
options: Continuous only if both are bijections.
questions.explanation: If $W$ is open in $Z$, then $(g \circ f)^{-1}(W) = f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$ is open because $g^{-1}(W)$ is open (continuity of $g$) and its preimage under $f$ is open (continuity of $f$). So compositions of continuous maps are continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The pasting lemma states that if $X = A \cup B$ with $A, B$ both closed, and $f : A \to Y$, $g : B \to Y$ are continuous and agree on $A \cap B$, then the combined map is continuous. What is the combined map?
options: A continuous map $h : X \to Y$ defined piecewise by $f$ and $g$.
questions.explanation: The pasting lemma produces a well-defined continuous map $h$ on all of $X$ by setting $h = f$ on $A$ and $h = g$ on $B$; agreement on the overlap makes it well defined, and the closedness of $A$ and $B$ makes it continuous. The overlap need not be empty.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following is a topological property (invariant under homeomorphism)?
questions.explanation: Compactness is preserved by homeomorphisms (indeed by any continuous surjection), so it is a topological property. Boundedness, diameter, and length depend on a metric or embedding and are not topological invariants.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the standard topology, which map exhibits a homeomorphism between the open interval $(-1, 1)$ and the whole real line $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The map $f(x) = x/(1 - x^2)$ is a continuous strictly increasing bijection from $(-1,1)$ onto $\mathbb{R}$ with continuous inverse, so it is a homeomorphism. The squaring and absolute value maps are not injective on $(-1,1)$, and $\sin$ is not injective there either.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Consider the identity map $\operatorname{id} : (\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{T}_{\text{discrete}}) \to (\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{T}_{\text{standard}})$. Is it continuous, and is it a homeomorphism?
options: It is continuous but not a homeomorphism.
It is neither continuous nor a bijection.
questions.explanation: The identity from a finer (discrete) topology to a coarser (standard) one is continuous, since every standard-open set is discrete-open. But the inverse, from standard to discrete, is not continuous (a singleton is discrete-open but not standard-open), so it is not a homeomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $f : X \to Y$ be continuous. Which containment relating images and closures always holds for a subset $A \subseteq X$?
questions.explanation: Continuity is equivalent to $f(\overline{A}) \subseteq \overline{f(A)}$ for all $A$. Equality can fail (for instance if $f$ is not a closed map), so only this one-sided containment holds in general.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The quotient topology on $Y = X / \sim$, induced by a surjection $q : X \to Y$, declares a set $U \subseteq Y$ open exactly when which holds?
options: $U$ is the image of an open set.
questions.explanation: In the quotient topology, $U \subseteq Y$ is open if and only if its preimage $q^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$. This is the finest topology on $Y$ making the quotient map $q$ continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A map $f : X \to Y$ is called an open map when which condition holds?
options: $f(U)$ is open in $Y$ for every open $U \subseteq X$.
questions.explanation: An open map sends open sets to open sets: $f(U)$ is open whenever $U$ is open. The condition on preimages defines continuity, and the condition on closed sets defines a closed map.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which statement gives a correct way to recognize a homeomorphism among continuous bijections?
options: Every continuous bijection is a homeomorphism.
A continuous bijection that is also injective is a homeomorphism.
A continuous bijection is a homeomorphism iff it is an open map (equivalently a closed map).
A continuous bijection is a homeomorphism iff its domain is connected.
questions.explanation: For a continuous bijection $f$, the inverse $f^{-1}$ is continuous exactly when $f$ takes open sets to open sets, i.e. $f$ is an open map (equivalently a closed map). Continuous bijections are not automatically homeomorphisms.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: On the real line with the standard topology, is the function $f(x) = x^3$ a homeomorphism of $\mathbb{R}$ onto $\mathbb{R}$?
options: No, its inverse is not continuous.
Yes, it is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse $x^{1/3}$.
questions.explanation: The cubing map is a strictly increasing continuous bijection of $\mathbb{R}$ onto $\mathbb{R}$, and its inverse $x \mapsto x^{1/3}$ is also continuous. Hence $f(x) = x^3$ is a homeomorphism of $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the closed interval $[0, 1]$ homeomorphic to the open interval $(0, 1)$ in the standard topology?
options: No, $[0,1]$ is compact while $(0,1)$ is not, and compactness is a topological invariant.
questions.explanation: The interval $[0,1]$ is compact (closed and bounded) but $(0,1)$ is not compact. Since compactness is preserved by homeomorphisms, no homeomorphism can exist between them. Length is not a topological invariant, so that is not the reason.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $Y$ carry the indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, Y\}$. Which maps $f : X \to Y$ from an arbitrary space $X$ are continuous?
options: Every map $f : X \to Y$ is continuous.
questions.explanation: When $Y$ is indiscrete, the only open sets are $\emptyset$ and $Y$, whose preimages are $\emptyset$ and $X$, both open in any $X$. So every map into an indiscrete space is continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of these properties is NOT preserved by a homeomorphism?
options: Being a bounded subset of a metric space.
questions.explanation: Boundedness is a metric notion, not a topological one: a homeomorphism can carry a bounded set to an unbounded one (for example $(-1,1)$ to $\mathbb{R}$). Connectedness, compactness, and the count of components are all topological invariants.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A continuous real-valued function $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$ is sometimes characterized using subbasic open sets. Which family suffices to test continuity into $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The open rays $(a, \infty)$ and $(-\infty, b)$ form a subbasis for the standard topology on $\mathbb{R}$, so $f$ is continuous iff each preimage $f^{-1}((a,\infty))$ and $f^{-1}((-\infty,b))$ is open. Testing on a subbasis is enough because preimages commute with unions and intersections.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ have topology $\mathcal{T}_X = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a, b\}, X\}$ and let $Y = \{0, 1\}$ have the discrete topology. Consider the map $f$ with $f(a) = 0$, $f(b) = 1$, $f(c) = 1$. Test continuity by checking preimages of the open sets of $Y$.
questions.solution: Since $Y$ is discrete, it suffices to check the preimages of the singletons. We have $f^{-1}(\{0\}) = \{a\}$, which is open in $X$. We have $f^{-1}(\{1\}) = \{b, c\}$; this set is open in $X$ exactly when it is listed in $\mathcal{T}_X$. The number of open sets of $X$ that the preimage $f^{-1}(\{1\})$ matches is {{1}}. Therefore $f$ {{2}} continuous.
questions.explanation: $f^{-1}(\{1\}) = \{b,c\}$ is not among the open sets $\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a,b\}, X$ (it matches $0$ of them), so its preimage is not open. Continuity fails because the preimage of an open set is not open.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Work with finite product topologies. Suppose $X$ has $3$ open sets and $Y$ has $2$ open sets (each topology listed explicitly). Count basic open rectangles, and recall the universal property of products.
questions.solution: A basis for the product topology on $X \times Y$ consists of rectangles $U \times V$ with $U$ open in $X$ and $V$ open in $Y$. The number of such products of listed open sets (including those involving the empty set) is $3 \times 2$, which equals {{1}}. A map into a product is continuous if and only if {{2}} of its coordinate functions are continuous.
questions.explanation: There are $3 \cdot 2 = 6$ products $U \times V$ from the listed open sets. By the universal property of the product topology, a map into a product is continuous exactly when all of its coordinate functions are continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Consider the two interval pairs in the standard topology: first $[0, 1]$ versus $(0, 1)$, and second $(-1, 1)$ versus $\mathbb{R}$. Decide homeomorphism using compactness.
questions.solution: The interval $[0,1]$ is compact but $(0,1)$ is not, and compactness is a topological invariant, so these two {{1}} homeomorphic. By contrast $(-1,1)$ and $\mathbb{R}$ are both noncompact, and the map $x \mapsto x/(1 - x^2)$ is a homeomorphism, so they {{2}} homeomorphic. The number of these two stated pairs that are homeomorphic is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $[0,1]$ (compact) cannot be homeomorphic to $(0,1)$ (noncompact). The open interval $(-1,1)$ is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}$ via $x \mapsto x/(1-x^2)$. So exactly $1$ of the two pairs is homeomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X$ be a set with the discrete topology and $Y$ a set with the indiscrete topology. Decide how many maps in each direction are guaranteed continuous, in terms of the open-set test.
questions.solution: Every subset of a discrete space is open, so for a map out of $X$ the preimage of any open set is automatically open; hence {{1}} maps from $X$ to any space are continuous. Likewise the only open sets of the indiscrete $Y$ are $\emptyset$ and $Y$, whose preimages are always open, so {{2}} maps into $Y$ are continuous. Combining, the identity-style obstruction to continuity in these two settings occurs in {{3}} cases.
questions.explanation: Maps out of a discrete space are all continuous (every preimage is open), and maps into an indiscrete space are all continuous (only $\emptyset$ and the whole space need open preimages). So there are $0$ obstructions in these two settings.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
